did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with transfer learning to classify skin cancer types from dermoscopic images. The model achieved 97.38% accuracy on a dataset of 10,015 images across 7 classes.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a CNN-based mobile application for skin cancer classification. It details specific methods (transfer learning, Keras, TFLite), a specific dataset (HAM10000, 10,015 images), and reports new empirical results (97.38% accuracy, precision/recall/F1 scores, and software testing metrics like UAT and black box testing).

title: Deteksi Awal Klasifikasi Jenis Penyakit Kanker Kulit Dengan Algoritma Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) Berbasis Mobile Apps
brief_summary: This study developed a mobile-based application for early detection and classification of skin cancer using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with transfer learning. The model was trained on the HAM10000 dataset, comprising 10,015 dermoscopic images across seven classes, split into 75% training and 25% testing data. The CNN architecture, implemented using Keras and converted to TensorFlow Lite for Android, achieved an accuracy of 97.38% and a validation accuracy of 97.39% after 200 epochs. Evaluation metrics showed an average precision, recall, and F1-score of 97%, representing a significant improvement over previous research that used fewer classes and images. Software testing, including white box, black box, and User Acceptance Testing (UAT), confirmed the application's functionality and user acceptance.

all_task_types.target: Seven classes of skin cancer/lesions (Melanocytic nevi, Melanoma, Benign keratosis-like lesions, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratoses, Vascular lesions, Dermatofibroma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of dermoscopic images into 7 distinct diagnostic categories using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). The model predicts discrete disease labels rather than continuous values. Evaluation metrics include accuracy (97.38%), precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset available on Kaggle.com, containing skin lesion images and associated metadata (lesion type, patient gender, lesion location).

use.training: Used for training the CNN model (75% of the dataset).
use.validation: Used for monitoring validation accuracy during training (specific count not reported, distinct from test set).
use.testing: Used for evaluating model generalization and calculating precision, recall, and F1-score (25% of the dataset).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No formal hypothesis testing was reported in the paper. The study reports performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and software testing results (white box, black box, UAT) but does not use statistical significance tests or p-values to compare results.

experiment.model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with transfer learning
experiment.notes: The model achieved an accuracy of 97.38% and validation accuracy of 97.39% after 200 epochs. The average precision, recall, and F1-score were 97%. This represents a significant increase from previous research that used fewer classes and images.

white_box_testing.interpretation: Program is not complex and easy to fix

Software Testing.notes: White box testing indicated low complexity. Black box testing showed 100% success rate. UAT showed 84% success rate.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning (specifically using a BiT model loaded from HDF5)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: BiT model (loaded from HDF5 for transfer learning)
conclusions: The CNN model with transfer learning achieved an accuracy of 97.38% and a validation accuracy of 97.39% after 200 epochs on the HAM10000 dataset.
The classification model demonstrated an average precision, recall, and F1-score of 97%.

The study improved upon previous research by increasing the number of classification classes from two to seven and the dataset size from 5,000 to 10,015 images, resulting in a more accurate and representative diagnosis.

Software testing results indicated that the program is not complex and easy to fix (white box testing), achieved a 100% success rate in black box testing, and an 84% success rate in User Acceptance Testing (UAT).
The developed mobile-based application facilitates faster and more efficient skin cancer diagnosis by helping to differentiate between malignant and non-malignant skin lesions.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have class imbalance and potential selection bias; no external validation on independent datasets was performed.
The sample size for User Acceptance Testing (UAT) and Black Box testing was extremely small (n=3 respondents), limiting the generalizability of the software usability and functional testing results.

The paper does not report per-class performance metrics (precision, recall, F1-score) for the seven classes, only providing an aggregate average of 97%, which masks potential performance disparities among imbalanced classes.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was conducted to validate the claimed 'significant increase' in performance compared to previous research.
The model was converted to TensorFlow Lite for Android deployment, but there is no evidence of clinical validation or comparison against dermatologist diagnoses in a real-world clinical setting.

Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; while a 75/25 split is mentioned, the paper does not confirm if patient-level separation was enforced to prevent image-level leakage from the same patient appearing in both train and test sets.
